From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Type of Ancient Egyptian sintered-quartz ceramic

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0e/Shawabti_of_Lady_Sati.jpg/250px-Shawabti_of_Lady_Sati.jpg)](/wiki/File:Shawabti_of_Lady_Sati.jpg)

Egyptian faience [ushabti](/wiki/Ushabti "Ushabti") of Lady Sati. [New Kingdom](/wiki/New_Kingdom_of_Egypt "New Kingdom of Egypt"), [Dynasty XVIII](/wiki/Eighteenth_dynasty_of_Egypt "Eighteenth dynasty of Egypt"), reign of [Amenhotep III](/wiki/Amenhotep_III "Amenhotep III"), c. 1390–1352 BC. Possibly from [Saqqara](/wiki/Saqqara "Saqqara").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/WLA_brooklynmuseum_Tile_Frieze_Representing_Lotus_and_Grape.jpg/250px-WLA_brooklynmuseum_Tile_Frieze_Representing_Lotus_and_Grape.jpg)](/wiki/File:WLA_brooklynmuseum_Tile_Frieze_Representing_Lotus_and_Grape.jpg)

Tile frieze with [lotus](/wiki/Nelumbo_nucifera "Nelumbo nucifera") and grapes

**Egyptian faience** is a [sintered](/wiki/Sintering "Sintering")-quartz [ceramic](/wiki/Ceramic "Ceramic") material from [Ancient Egypt](/wiki/Ancient_Egypt "Ancient Egypt"). The [sintering](/wiki/Sintering "Sintering") process "covered [the material] with a true vitreous coating" as the [quartz](/wiki/Quartz "Quartz") underwent [vitrification](/wiki/Vitrification "Vitrification"), creating a bright lustre of various colours "usually in a transparent blue or green isotropic glass". Its name in the [Ancient Egyptian language](/wiki/Ancient_Egyptian_language "Ancient Egyptian language") was ***tjehenet***,[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) literally "the gleaming" or "the dazzling",[[3]](#cite_note-3) and modern archeological terms for it include **sintered quartz**, **glazed frit**, and **glazed composition**.[[4]](#cite_note-British_Museum-4) *Tjehenet* is distinct from the crystalline pigment [Egyptian blue](/wiki/Egyptian_blue "Egyptian blue"),[[5]](#cite_note-Grose-1999-5) for which it has sometimes incorrectly been used as a synonym.[[4]](#cite_note-British_Museum-4)

It is not [faience](/wiki/Faience "Faience") in the usual sense of [tin-glazed](/wiki/Tin-glazed "Tin-glazed") pottery, and is different from the enormous range of clay-based [Ancient Egyptian pottery](/wiki/Ancient_Egyptian_pottery "Ancient Egyptian pottery"), from which utilitarian vessels were made. It is similar to later Islamic [stonepaste](/wiki/Stonepaste "Stonepaste") (or "fritware") from the [Middle East](/wiki/Middle_East "Middle East"), although that generally includes more clay.[[4]](#cite_note-British_Museum-4)

Egyptian faience is considerably more porous than glass proper. It can be cast in molds to create small vessels, jewelry and decorative objects.[[5]](#cite_note-Grose-1999-5) Although it contains the major constituents of [glass](/wiki/Glass "Glass") (silica, lime) and no [clay](/wiki/Clay "Clay") until late periods, Egyptian faience is frequently discussed in surveys of ancient pottery, as in stylistic and art-historical terms, objects made of it are closer to pottery styles than [ancient Egyptian glass](/wiki/Ancient_Egyptian_glass "Ancient Egyptian glass").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/Faience_Vessel_with_Procession_of_Four_Bulls%2C_ca._775-653_B.C.E._%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Faience_Vessel_with_Procession_of_Four_Bulls%2C_ca._775-653_B.C.E._%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Faience_Vessel_with_Procession_of_Four_Bulls,_ca._775-653_B.C.E._(cropped).jpg)

Faience vessel with procession of four bulls, c. 775–653 BCE [Brooklyn Museum](/wiki/Brooklyn_Museum "Brooklyn Museum")

Egyptian faience was very widely used for small objects, from beads to small statues, and is found in both elite and popular contexts. It was the most common material for [scarabs](/wiki/Scarab_(artifact) "Scarab (artifact)") and other forms of amulet and [ushabti](/wiki/Ushabti "Ushabti") figures, and it was used in most forms of ancient Egyptian jewellery, as the glaze made it smooth against the skin. Larger applications included dishware, such as cups and bowls, and [wall tiles](/wiki/Wall_tile "Wall tile"), which were mostly used for temples.[[6]](#cite_note-6) The well-known blue [hippopotamus](/wiki/Hippopotamus "Hippopotamus") figurines, placed in the tombs of officials, can be up to 20 cm (7.9 in) long,[[7]](#cite_note-7) approaching the maximum practical size for Egyptian faience, though the [Victoria and Albert Museum](/wiki/Victoria_and_Albert_Museum "Victoria and Albert Museum") in London has a 215.9-centimetre (85.0 in) [sceptre](/wiki/Sceptre "Sceptre"), dated 1427–1400 BC.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

## Scope of the term

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Egyptian_faience&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Scope of the term")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Egyptian_-_Group_of_16_Amulets_Strung_as_a_Necklace_-_Walters_481685-1699_-_View_A.jpg/250px-Egyptian_-_Group_of_16_Amulets_Strung_as_a_Necklace_-_Walters_481685-1699_-_View_A.jpg)](/wiki/File:Egyptian_-_Group_of_16_Amulets_Strung_as_a_Necklace_-_Walters_481685-1699_-_View_A.jpg)

Group of 16 amulets strung as a necklace, in the typical bright faience blue, [Late Period](/wiki/Late_Period_of_ancient_Egypt "Late Period of ancient Egypt")

It is called "Egyptian faience" to distinguish it from [faience](/wiki/Faience "Faience"), the [tin-glazed pottery](/wiki/Tin-glazed_pottery "Tin-glazed pottery") whose name came from [Faenza](/wiki/Faenza "Faenza") in northern Italy,[[9]](#cite_note-Nicholson_and_Peltenburg-9) a center of [maiolica](/wiki/Maiolica "Maiolica") (one type of faience) production in the late [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"). Egyptian faience was both exported widely in the ancient world and made locally in many places, and is found in [Mesopotamia](/wiki/Mesopotamia "Mesopotamia"), around the Mediterranean and in northern Europe as far away as [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland"). The term is used for the material wherever it was made and modern scientific analyses are often the only way of establishing the provenance of simple objects such as the very common beads.[[9]](#cite_note-Nicholson_and_Peltenburg-9)[[10]](#cite_note-Stone_and_Thomas_1956-10)

The term is therefore unsatisfactory in several respects, although clear in an Ancient Egyptian context, and is increasingly rejected in museum and archaeological usage. The [British Museum](/wiki/British_Museum "British Museum") now calls this material "glazed composition", with the following note in their online collection database:

> The term is used for objects with a body made of finely powdered quartz grains fused together with small amounts of alkali and/or lime through partial heating. The bodies are usually colourless but natural impurities give them a brown or greyish tint. Colourants can also be added to give it an artificial colour. It can be modelled by hand, thrown or moulded, and hardens with firing. This material is used in the context of Islamic ceramics where it is described as stonepaste (or fritware). Glazed composition is related to glass, but glass is formed by completely fusing the ingredients in a liquid melted at high temperature. This material is also popularly called faience in the contexts of Ancient Egypt and Ancient Near East. However, this is a misnomer as these objects have no relationship to the glazed pottery vessels made in Faenza, from which the faience term derives. Other authors use the terms sintered quartz, glazed frit, frit, composition, Egyptian Blue, paste or (in the 19th century) even porcelain, although the last two terms are very inappropriate as they also describe imitation gems and a type of ceramic. [Frit](/wiki/Frit "Frit") is technically a flux.[[4]](#cite_note-British_Museum-4)

## Glazes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Egyptian_faience&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Glazes")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/71/Kneeling_Statue_of_Nesbanebdjedet%2C_ca._755-730_B.C.E.%2C37.344E.jpg/250px-Kneeling_Statue_of_Nesbanebdjedet%2C_ca._755-730_B.C.E.%2C37.344E.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kneeling_Statue_of_Nesbanebdjedet,_ca._755-730_B.C.E.,37.344E.jpg)

Kneeling Statue of Nesbanebdjedet, ca. 755-730 BC, now 13.8 cm high

From the inception of faience in the archaeological record of [Ancient Egypt](/wiki/Ancient_Egypt "Ancient Egypt"), the elected colors of the [glazes](/wiki/Ceramic_glaze "Ceramic glaze") varied within an array of blue-green hues. Glazed in these colours, faience was perceived as substitute for blue-green materials such as [turquoise](/wiki/Turquoise "Turquoise"), found in the [Sinai Peninsula](/wiki/Sinai_Peninsula "Sinai Peninsula"), and [lapis lazuli](/wiki/Lapis_lazuli "Lapis lazuli") from [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan").[[9]](#cite_note-Nicholson_and_Peltenburg-9) According to the archaeologist David Frederick Grose, the quest to imitate precious stones "explains why most all early glasses are opaque and brilliantly colored" and that the deepest blue color imitating lapis lazuli was likely the most sought after.[[11]](#cite_note-11) As early as the Predynastic graves at [Naqada](/wiki/Naqada "Naqada"), Badar, el-Amrah, Matmar, Harageh, Avadiyedh and El-Gerzeh, glazed [steatite](/wiki/Steatite "Steatite") and faience beads are found associated with these semi-precious stones.[[12]](#cite_note-Nicholson-12) The association of faience with turquoise and lapis lazuli becomes even more conspicuous in Quennou's funerary papyrus, giving his title as the director of overseer of faience-making, using the word which strictly means lapis lazuli, which by the [New Kingdom](/wiki/New_Kingdom_of_Egypt "New Kingdom of Egypt") had also come to refer to the 'substitute', faience.[[9]](#cite_note-Nicholson_and_Peltenburg-9) The symbolism embedded in blue glazing could recall both the [Nile](/wiki/Nile "Nile"), the waters of [heaven](/wiki/Heaven "Heaven") and the home of the [gods](/wiki/God "God"), whereas green could possibly evoke images of regeneration, rebirth and vegetation.[[13]](#cite_note-Friedman-13)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/Beadnet_dress-27.1548.1-2-IMG_4582-gradient.jpg/250px-Beadnet_dress-27.1548.1-2-IMG_4582-gradient.jpg)](/wiki/File:Beadnet_dress-27.1548.1-2-IMG_4582-gradient.jpg)

Egyptian faience beaded fishnet dress dating from the [Fourth Dynasty of Egypt](/wiki/Fourth_Dynasty_of_Egypt "Fourth Dynasty of Egypt").

### Relationship with Egyptian copper industry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Egyptian_faience&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Relationship with Egyptian copper industry")]

The discovery of faience glazing has tentatively been associated with the [copper](/wiki/Copper "Copper") industry: [bronze](/wiki/Bronze "Bronze") scale and corrosion products of [leaded copper](/wiki/Leaded_copper "Leaded copper") objects are found in the manufacture of faience [pigments](/wiki/Pigment "Pigment").[[14]](#cite_note-Kaczamrcyz_and_Hedges-14) However, although the likelihood of glazed [quartz](/wiki/Quartz "Quartz") pebbles developing accidentally in traces in copper [smelting](/wiki/Smelting "Smelting") [furnaces](/wiki/Metallurgical_furnace "Metallurgical furnace") from the copper and wood ash is high, the regions in which these processes originate do not coincide.[[15]](#cite_note-Petrie-15)

### Relationship with Egyptian glass industry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Egyptian_faience&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Relationship with Egyptian glass industry")]

Although it appears that no [glass](/wiki/Glass "Glass") was intentionally produced in Egypt before the [Eighteenth Dynasty](/wiki/Eighteenth_Dynasty_of_Egypt "Eighteenth Dynasty of Egypt") (as the establishment of glass manufacture is generally attributed to the reign of [Thutmose III](/wiki/Thutmose_III "Thutmose III")), it is likely that faience, [frit](/wiki/Frit "Frit") and glass were all made in close proximity or in the same workshop complex, since developments in one industry are reflected in others.[[12]](#cite_note-Nicholson-12) Such close relationship is reflected in the prominent similarity of the formulations of faience glaze and contemporary glass compositions.[[14]](#cite_note-Kaczamrcyz_and_Hedges-14) Despite the differences in the pyrotechnology of glass and faience, faience being worked cold, archaeological evidence suggests that New Kingdom glass and faience production was undertaken in the same workshops.[[9]](#cite_note-Nicholson_and_Peltenburg-9)

## Production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Egyptian_faience&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Production")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5f/Egyptian_faience_beads.jpg/250px-Egyptian_faience_beads.jpg)](/wiki/File:Egyptian_faience_beads.jpg)

Assortment of Egyptian faience beads

### Typical composition and access to raw materials

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Egyptian_faience&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Typical composition and access to raw materials")]

Faience has been defined as the first high technology ceramic, to emphasize its status as an artificial medium, rendering it effectively a precious stone.[[16]](#cite_note-Vandiver-16) Egyptian faience is a non-clay based ceramic composed of crushed quartz or sand, with small amounts of [calcite](/wiki/Calcite "Calcite") [lime](/wiki/Lime_(material) "Lime (material)") and a mixture of [alkalis](/wiki/Alkali "Alkali"), displaying surface [vitrification](/wiki/Glass_transition "Glass transition") due to the [soda lime](/wiki/Soda-lime_glass "Soda-lime glass") [silica](/wiki/Silica "Silica") [glaze](/wiki/Ceramic_glaze "Ceramic glaze") often containing copper pigments to create a bright blue-green luster.[[9]](#cite_note-Nicholson_and_Peltenburg-9) While in most instances domestic ores seem to have provided the bulk of the mineral pigments, evidence suggests that during periods of prosperity, raw materials not available locally, such as lead and copper, were imported.[[14]](#cite_note-Kaczamrcyz_and_Hedges-14) Plant ash, from "[halophyte](/wiki/Halophyte "Halophyte")" (salt-tolerant) plants typical of dry and sea areas, was the major source of alkali until the Ptolemaic Period, when [natron](/wiki/Natron "Natron")-based alkalis almost completely replaced the previous source.[[16]](#cite_note-Vandiver-16) Although the chemical composition of faience materials varies over time and according to the status of the workshop, also as a cause of change of accessibility of raw materials, the material constitution of the glaze is at all times consistent with the generally accepted version of faience glazing.[[14]](#cite_note-Kaczamrcyz_and_Hedges-14)

### Faience working technology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Egyptian_faience&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Faience working technology")]

Typical faience mixture is [thixotropic](/wiki/Thixotropic "Thixotropic"), that is thick at first and then soft and flowing as it begins to be formed.[[17]](#cite_note-Kiefer_and_Allibert'-17)
This property, together with the angularity of silica particles, accounts for the gritty slumps formed when the material is wetted, rendering faience a difficult material to hold a shape.[[18]](#cite_note-Noble'-18) If pressed too vigorously, this material will resist flow until it yields and cracks, due to its limited plastic deformation and low yield strength.[[16]](#cite_note-Vandiver-16)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/Egyptian_-_Vase_with_Names_of_Amenhotep_III_and_Queen_Tiye_-_Walters_48403.jpg/250px-Egyptian_-_Vase_with_Names_of_Amenhotep_III_and_Queen_Tiye_-_Walters_48403.jpg)](/wiki/File:Egyptian_-_Vase_with_Names_of_Amenhotep_III_and_Queen_Tiye_-_Walters_48403.jpg)

This faience vessel from the reign of [Amenhotep III](/wiki/Amenhotep_III "Amenhotep III") exhibits two different shades of blue that can be achieved by adding [cobalt](/wiki/Cobalt "Cobalt") and [copper](/wiki/Copper "Copper") pigments to faience paste. [Walters Art Museum](/wiki/Walters_Art_Museum "Walters Art Museum"), [Baltimore](/wiki/Baltimore "Baltimore").

### Body binding technology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Egyptian_faience&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Body binding technology")]

A number of possible binding agents, amongst [Arabic gum](/wiki/Arabic_gum "Arabic gum"), [clay](/wiki/Clay "Clay"), [lime](/wiki/Lime_(material) "Lime (material)"), [egg white](/wiki/Egg_white "Egg white") and [resin](/wiki/Resin "Resin"), have been suggested to help in the binding process.[[19]](#cite_note-Lucas_and_Harris'-19) Although traces of clay have been found in most Pharaonic faience, reconstruction experiments showed that clay, [organic gums](/w/index.php?title=Organic_gum&action=edit&redlink=1 "Organic gum (page does not exist)") or lime while successfully improving the wet working performance, failed to improve the fired strength of the faience, or proved the gum was too sticky for the removal of objects from their molds.[[9]](#cite_note-Nicholson_and_Peltenburg-9)[[16]](#cite_note-Vandiver-16)[[19]](#cite_note-Lucas_and_Harris'-19) The use of alkalis as binders, in the form of natron or plant ash, produced suitable results in experiments.[[9]](#cite_note-Nicholson_and_Peltenburg-9) Pulverized glass or sintered material of similar composition could also enhance the fired strength of faience bodies: the compositions of such glasses is in fact comparable to the published compositions of New Kingdom glass.[[14]](#cite_note-Kaczamrcyz_and_Hedges-14)[[20]](#cite_note-Kühne,_K.'-20)

### Body working technology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Egyptian_faience&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Body working technology")]

Three methods have been hypothesized to shape the body of faience objects: modeling, moulding and abrasion, the last being used in conjunction with the first two.[[9]](#cite_note-Nicholson_and_Peltenburg-9) Modeling, scraping and grinding are the techniques most widely used in earlier times, as represented in the material qualities of Predynastic and Protodynastic faience objects.[[12]](#cite_note-Nicholson-12) Predynastic bead manufacture is essentially a cold technology, more akin to stone working than glass: a general form of faience is modeled, possibly free formed by hand, then holes are drilled to create beads.[[12]](#cite_note-Nicholson-12)
In the [Middle Kingdom](/wiki/Middle_Kingdom_of_Egypt "Middle Kingdom of Egypt"), the techniques employed are molding and forming on a core, sometimes in conjunction with intermediate layers between the glaze and the body.[[16]](#cite_note-Vandiver-16) Marbleized faience, resulting from the working of different colored faience bodies together, so as to produce a uniform adherent body, also appears in this period.[[14]](#cite_note-Kaczamrcyz_and_Hedges-14)[[16]](#cite_note-Vandiver-16) Towards the end of the Middle Kingdom, incising, inlaying and resisting techniques appear: these were bound to become progressively popular towards the New Kingdom.[[16]](#cite_note-Vandiver-16) In the New Kingdom, beads, amulets and finger rings are produced by a combination of modeling and molding techniques.[[16]](#cite_note-Vandiver-16) In this period, sculptural detail is created using inlays of different colored faience or by scraping the body to reveal the white intermediate layer in relief.[[16]](#cite_note-Vandiver-16) Moulding was first applied to faience manufacture in the Middle Kingdom by forming a model of an object, or employing a finished faience piece, impressing it in wet clay, and later by firing the clay to create a durable mold.[[12]](#cite_note-Nicholson-12)[[21]](#cite_note-Boyce,_A.'-21) The faience paste could then be pressed into the mold, and following drying, be re-worked through surface abrasion before firing.[[12]](#cite_note-Nicholson-12) Moulds could facilitate mass production of faience objects such as amulets rings and inlays, as evidenced by the several thousand of small open face, earth-ware clay molds excavated at Tell el Amarna.[[15]](#cite_note-Petrie-15) The level of standardisation that use of moulds produced varied, with a compositional and morphological study of faience ushabtis suggested that mass-production is an oversimplification of a complex process that may more accurately described as batch-processing.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/Applique_in_faience_per_tempietti_in_legno%2C_periodo_tolemaico%2C_falco_05.jpg/250px-Applique_in_faience_per_tempietti_in_legno%2C_periodo_tolemaico%2C_falco_05.jpg)](/wiki/File:Applique_in_faience_per_tempietti_in_legno,_periodo_tolemaico,_falco_05.jpg)

Falcon, [Ptolemaic period](/wiki/Ptolemaic_period "Ptolemaic period"), 13.5 cm high, see text

Wheel throwing, possibly occurring from the New Kingdom onwards, is certainly established by the Greco-Roman period, when large amounts of clay seem to have been added to the faience body.[[12]](#cite_note-Nicholson-12) Because of the limited plasticity of faience, rendering throwing extremely difficult, a progressive increase of clay in the faience bodies culminating in the quartz, clay and glass frit bodies of Islamic times, is observed in the archaeological record.[[16]](#cite_note-Vandiver-16)[[23]](#cite_note-Kiefer_'-23)

[Ptolemaic](/wiki/Ptolemaic_period "Ptolemaic period") and [Roman](/wiki/Roman_Egypt "Roman Egypt") faience tends to be typologically and technologically distinct from the earlier material: it is characterized by the widespread use of moulding and high relief on vessels.[[24]](#cite_note-Shortland_and_Tite'-24) A very unusual and finely made group of figures of deities and falcons in the [Metropolitan Museum of Art](/wiki/Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art "Metropolitan Museum of Art") in New York, apparently representing [hieroglyphs](/wiki/Hieroglyph "Hieroglyph") that are elements from a royal inscription, perhaps from a wooden shrine, is decorated in a form of [champlevé](/wiki/Champlev%C3%A9 "Champlevé") (typically a technique for [enamel](/wiki/Vitreous_enamel "Vitreous enamel") on metal). Depressions in the faience body were filled with coloured "vitreous pastes" and refired, followed by polishing.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

Polychrome pieces were usually made by inlaying different colours of paste.[[26]](#cite_note-inlay-26)

## Glazing technology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Egyptian_faience&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Glazing technology")]

The technology of glazing a siliceous body with a soda lime silica glaze employs various methods discovered over time: namely application, efflorescence and cementation glazing.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/Louvre_Egyptologie_-_Vases_Ramses_II.jpg/250px-Louvre_Egyptologie_-_Vases_Ramses_II.jpg)](/wiki/File:Louvre_Egyptologie_-_Vases_Ramses_II.jpg)

Vases probably used for the funeral of [Rameses II](/wiki/Rameses_II "Rameses II")

### Application glazing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Egyptian_faience&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Application glazing")]

In the application method, formerly assumed to be the only one used for faience glazing; silica, lime and alkalis are ground in the raw state to a small particle size, thus mixed in water to form a slurry which is then applied to the quartz core.[[12]](#cite_note-Nicholson-12)[[15]](#cite_note-Petrie-15)[[19]](#cite_note-Lucas_and_Harris'-19) Partial [fritting](/wiki/Fritted_glass "Fritted glass") of the slurry favors the first stages of vitrification, which in turn lowers the final firing temperature. The slurry can be then applied to the body, through brushing or dipping, to create a fine, powdery coating.[[16]](#cite_note-Vandiver-16) Upon firing, the water from the melting glaze partially diffuses in the sand body, sintering the quartz particles and thus creating some solid bridges in the body.[[16]](#cite_note-Vandiver-16)

### Efflorescence glazing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Egyptian_faience&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Efflorescence glazing")]

In the self-glazing process of efflorescence, the glazing materials, in the form of water-soluble alkali salts, are mixed with the raw crushed quartz of the core of the object.[[17]](#cite_note-Kiefer_and_Allibert'-17)[[18]](#cite_note-Noble'-18)[[27]](#cite_note-Binns'-27) As the water in the body evaporates, the salts migrate to the surface of the object to recrystallize, creating a thin surface, which glazes upon firing.[[16]](#cite_note-Vandiver-16)

### Cementation glazing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Egyptian_faience&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Cementation glazing")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/Louvre_calice_forme_nenuphar.JPG/250px-Louvre_calice_forme_nenuphar.JPG)](/wiki/File:Louvre_calice_forme_nenuphar.JPG)

Cup with [lotus](/wiki/Egyptian_lotus "Egyptian lotus") decoration, 14.4 cm high

Cementation glazing, a technique discovered in the Middle Kingdom, is also a self-glazing technique.[[12]](#cite_note-Nicholson-12) The possibility of the existence of cementation glazing, also known as '[Qom technique](/wiki/Qom_technique "Qom technique")', followed the observation of this method being used in the city of [Qom](/wiki/Qom "Qom") in Iran in the 1960s.[[28]](#cite_note-Wulff_et_Al.,_1968'-28) In this method the artifact, while buried in a glazing powder with a high flux content, is heated inside a vessel, causing the fusion of the object with the cement. During firing, the flux migrates to the quartz and combines with it to form a glassy coating.[[28]](#cite_note-Wulff_et_Al.,_1968'-28)

### Alternative techniques

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Egyptian_faience&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Alternative techniques")]

A vapour glaze reaction similar to salt glazing, as an alternative glazing process, has been suggested. In this process, the vaporization or dissociation of salts leads to vapour transport through the enveloping powder to the quartz body where a glaze is formed.[[29]](#cite_note-Williamson_1942-29)

### Recognition of glazing techniques

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Egyptian_faience&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Recognition of glazing techniques")]

Although glaze compositions vary regionally and chronologically, depending on the formation of the body and the glazing process employed, objects produced with different glazing techniques do not exhibit immediate diagnostic chemical variations in their compositions.[[14]](#cite_note-Kaczamrcyz_and_Hedges-14)[[30]](#cite_note-Tite_et_Al._1983'-30) The recognition of the various glazing techniques, through microscopic observations of the degree of sintering and the vitreous phase of quartz body, is also ambiguous. For instance, objects with applied glazes and those which may have been glazed by efflorescence have overlapping characteristic features.[[14]](#cite_note-Kaczamrcyz_and_Hedges-14)[[31]](#cite_note-Tite_and_Bimson_1986'-31) The following proposed criteria are subject to variation caused by increases in flux concentration, firing temperatures and time at peak temperatures.[[16]](#cite_note-Vandiver-16)

*Recognition of application glazing*- Macroscopically, applied glazes vary in thickness through the body, displaying thicker glazes on bases.[[31]](#cite_note-Tite_and_Bimson_1986'-31) The traces of kiln supports, as well as the characteristic tendency to run and drip leading to pooling, may indicate the orientation of the object during firing.[[16]](#cite_note-Vandiver-16) In high magnification observations, the interface boundary of body and glaze appears well defined.[[16]](#cite_note-Vandiver-16) The absence of interstitial glass in the core is characteristic of application glazing: however, the possibility of adding glazing mixture to the quartz sand body, as well as the use of pre-melted glazes in the later periods, can predictably increase the degree of sintering of the core.[[24]](#cite_note-Shortland_and_Tite'-24)[[31]](#cite_note-Tite_and_Bimson_1986'-31)

*Recognition of cementation*- Objects glazed through cementation display a thin even glaze all over the body, with no drying or firing marks, and portray a fairly friable and soft body.[[27]](#cite_note-Binns'-27)[[31]](#cite_note-Tite_and_Bimson_1986'-31) Microscopically, the concentration of copper characteristically decreases from the surface: the interaction layer is thin and well defined and the interstitial glass is absent with exception to the vicinity of the boundary layer.[[16]](#cite_note-Vandiver-16)[[31]](#cite_note-Tite_and_Bimson_1986'-31)

*Recognition of efflorescence glazing*- Pieces glazed by efflorescence may show traces of stand marks: the glaze appears thick and prone to cracking, thinning toward the edge of the piece and in concave areas.[[31]](#cite_note-Tite_and_Bimson_1986'-31) In high magnification the interstitial glass is extensive; the unreacted salts which have not reached the surface fuse of the body accumulate in the core, creating bridges between the quartz particles.[[31]](#cite_note-Tite_and_Bimson_1986'-31)

## Typologies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Egyptian_faience&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Typologies")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/Medio_regno%2C_XII-XVII_dinastia%2C_ippopotamo_in_faience%2C_1938-1539_ac_ca._01.JPG/250px-Medio_regno%2C_XII-XVII_dinastia%2C_ippopotamo_in_faience%2C_1938-1539_ac_ca._01.JPG)](/wiki/File:Medio_regno,_XII-XVII_dinastia,_ippopotamo_in_faience,_1938-1539_ac_ca._01.JPG)

[Hippopotamus](/wiki/Hippopotamus "Hippopotamus") figure for a tomb, [Middle Kingdom](/wiki/Middle_Kingdom_of_Egypt "Middle Kingdom of Egypt")

An extensive literature has accumulated in attempt to explain the processing of Egyptian faience and develop an adequate typology that encompasses both technological choices and chemical variations of faience bodies.[[12]](#cite_note-Nicholson-12)[[19]](#cite_note-Lucas_and_Harris'-19)[[31]](#cite_note-Tite_and_Bimson_1986'-31)[[32]](#cite_note-Brill_1999'-32) Body color, density and luster provided the basis of the first typology developed for faience: seven variants were proposed by Lucas and Harris and still permit the archaeologist to distinguish faience objects during field sorting.[[19]](#cite_note-Lucas_and_Harris'-19)

### Classification of body variants

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Egyptian_faience&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Classification of body variants")]

Most of the seven variants introduced by Lucas fail to recognize the glazing technology utilized or to suggest the stylistic and technological choices embedded in the manufacture of a faience object. However, variant A describes a technologically unique product and as such is still applicable: it has a finely ground underglaze consisting of quartz particles in a glass matrix, often revealed by incisions or depressions cut into the overlying glaze.[[14]](#cite_note-Kaczamrcyz_and_Hedges-14)[[24]](#cite_note-Shortland_and_Tite'-24) Glassy faience, variant E, displays no distinct outer layer from the interior, thus it has been suggested that the term 'faience' is a misnomer and the alternative name 'imperfect glass' has been advised.[[14]](#cite_note-Kaczamrcyz_and_Hedges-14) Regarding variant F specimens, Lucas suggests the use of lead glazes, however it appears that lead glazing was never practiced in Ancient Egypt.[[14]](#cite_note-Kaczamrcyz_and_Hedges-14)[[31]](#cite_note-Tite_and_Bimson_1986'-31)

## Workshop evidence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Egyptian_faience&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Workshop evidence")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/PalaceInlays-NubiansPhilistineAmoriteSyrianAndHittite-Compilation-MuseumOfFineArtsBoston.png/250px-PalaceInlays-NubiansPhilistineAmoriteSyrianAndHittite-Compilation-MuseumOfFineArtsBoston.png)](/wiki/File:PalaceInlays-NubiansPhilistineAmoriteSyrianAndHittite-Compilation-MuseumOfFineArtsBoston.png)

[Ramesses III prisoner tiles](/wiki/Ramesses_III_prisoner_tiles "Ramesses III prisoner tiles"): Inlay figures, faience and glass, of "the traditional enemies of Ancient Egypt" from a royal palace of [Ramesses III](/wiki/Ramesses_III "Ramesses III") (1182-1151 B.C.), at the [Museum of Fine Arts, Boston](/wiki/Museum_of_Fine_Arts,_Boston "Museum of Fine Arts, Boston"). From left: 2 [Nubians](/wiki/Nubians "Nubians"), [Philistine](/wiki/Philistine "Philistine"), [Amorite](/wiki/Amorite "Amorite"), Syrian, [Hittite](/wiki/Hittites "Hittites")

The excavations led by Petrie at Tell-Amarna and Naucratis have reported finding workshop evidence.[[15]](#cite_note-Petrie-15)
Nicholson explains, however, that while a square furnace-like structure at Amarna may be related to faience production, Petrie did not encounter any actual faience kilns at the site.[[33]](#cite_note-33) Lucas documented a large number of molds at the palace area of Amenhotep III at [Qantir](/wiki/Qantir "Qantir"), from 19th to 20th Dynasties, and at the palace area of Naucratis, also described in different sources as a scarab maker's and faience factory.[[15]](#cite_note-Petrie-15)[[19]](#cite_note-Lucas_and_Harris'-19) However, seeing there is a lack of carefully documented archaeological evidence as to the nature of faience factory sites, direct information about the glazing process does not exist.

Although recent excavations at the archaeological sites of Abydos and Amarna have supplemented our knowledge of the ancient production of faience gained from the earlier excavated sites of Lisht, Memphis and Naukratis, the differentiation of glass furnaces from faience kilns still remains problematic.[[12]](#cite_note-Nicholson-12) Replication experiments, using modern kilns and replica faience pastes, indicate that faience is fired in the range of 800–1000 °C[[34]](#cite_note-Verges_1992.'-34)[[35]](#cite_note-Stocks_1997'-35)

## Current use

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Egyptian_faience&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Current use")]

A number of ceramists are experimenting with Egyptian faience,[[36]](#cite_note-36) though some of the compositions bear only a passing resemblance to the original Egyptian formulae. There has also been a recent interest in the use of Egyptian faience in [3-d printing](/wiki/3-d_printing "3-d printing") technology.[[37]](#cite_note-3D-37) It may be possible to fire faience-like materials in a microwave.[[37]](#cite_note-3D-37)
